blocks.content: They stopped at a pull-off just before the three-hour mark.

Cassandra had spotted it from the road — a gap in the guardrail, a gravel shoulder, and beyond it a view the highway had been keeping secret: a wide valley laid out below them in the full mid-morning light, the kind of bright and open that makes everything look like it was placed there on purpose. A town in the low center of it, a thread of water catching the sun, the mountains rising clean against a sky so blue it seemed unreasonable.

They got out. The mountain air at this elevation was warm with the sun on it but still had a clean edge — the kind of day you want to stay in. Cassandra put her hands in her jacket pockets and walked to the edge of the gravel, and looked.
Ethan came and stood beside her.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Don't make a thing of it. Just take the picture.
He extended his arm. She pressed into his side. He pointed the camera at them — behind them, the valley and the mountains and the bright unreasonable sky — and he took it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I know. We look it, though. It's different.
She opened her phone and sent it to the family group chat.
Her father's response came before they'd gotten back to the car.
She got back in the car smiling.
They were maybe forty minutes from the inn when she made him pull over again.
No overlook this time, no particular reason — just a stretch of road where the trees broke and the light hit something right, and she said stop before she'd fully decided why.
They got out. Took a few pictures. No agenda. Just standing on the shoulder of a mountain road in the morning, being newlyweds.
She looked at the results and sent one to nobody.
Forty minutes later, the inn appeared.
